coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots-polymer composites with solid-state photoluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Xuegang
Ding, Guqiao
Wang, Hailong
Cui, Guangliang
Zhang, Pinhua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) and CDs-polymer composites were one-step synthesized in a hydrothermal process using hexamethylenetetramine and polycarbonate as precursors. Their optical properties and the application as solid-state phosphors towards white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were thoroughly investigated. CDs solutions exhibit strong yellow emission under blue illumination and possess excitation-independent luminescent behavior. Owing to precursor polymerization, CDs-polymer composites were synthesized in one step. The dried CDs-polymer composite powders exhibit excellent optical stability and no luminescence quenching was observed. Under light blue illumination, the powders show broad yellow luminescence with the peak wavelength centered at 550 nm and PL quantum yield of 14.86%. For their practical applications, stable white light emission was demonstrated by coating a 455 nm blue LED with the powders. Our research offers a valuable reference for growing CDs-polymer composites in a facile, low-cost and low-temperature hydrothermal process and developing white LEDs with light blue LEDs and metal-free phosphors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Luminescence
Phosphors
Hydrothermal method
Solid-state photoluminescence
White light-emitting diodes (LEDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Image of the product. (b) TEM image of CDs in limpid layer. (c) High-resolution TEM image of a carbon dot. (d) TEM image of large particles in turbid layer.
Characterizations of CDs, (a) FTIR spectrum. (b and c) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and N1s. (d) PL spectra.

Characterizations of CDs-Polymer composite powders, (a-c) UV–Vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra, respectively. (d) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve. The red line represents single exponential fit to the decay curve. (e) Spectrum of blue LED (∼455 nm) with the powders applied as phosphors. Inset: photograph of a working device. (b) The CIE chromaticity coordinates for the illuminating blue LED with powders coating. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots-polymer composites with solid-state photoluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Linyi University, Linyi 276005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Shanghai 200050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Qufu Normal University, Qufu 273165, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) and CDs-polymer composites were one-step synthesized in a hydrothermal process using hexamethylenetetramine and polycarbonate as precursors. Their optical properties and the application as solid-state phosphors towards white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were thoroughly investigated. CDs solutions exhibit strong yellow emission under blue illumination and possess excitation-independent luminescent behavior. Owing to precursor polymerization, CDs-polymer composites were synthesized in one step. The dried CDs-polymer composite powders exhibit excellent optical stability and no luminescence quenching was observed. Under light blue illumination, the powders show broad yellow luminescence with the peak wavelength centered at 550 nm and PL quantum yield of 14.86%. For their practical applications, stable white light emission was demonstrated by coating a 455 nm blue LED with the powders. Our research offers a valuable reference for growing CDs-polymer composites in a facile, low-cost and low-temperature hydrothermal process and developing white LEDs with light blue LEDs and metal-free phosphors.

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have recently received much attention because they offer many advantages over conventional incandescent and fluorescent lamps owing to their low power consumption, high efficiency, long working lifetime, reliability, and so forth. Common commercial white LEDs involve blue-LED chips combined with Ce3+-doped yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG/Ce) phosphors. However, the rare-earth-based phosphors have obvious defects such as high cost and toxicity. Now water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) are becoming one of the most promising metal-free phosphor nanomaterials due to their low cost and environment-friendly properties [1], but solid-state CDs usually represent aggregation-induced luminescence quenching while drying colloidal CDs into films or powders [2]. Obviously, the absence of luminescence in the solid state is a critical challenge hindering CDs from white light-emitting devices applications. To date, a few solid-state luminescence of CDs has mainly been achieved by dispersing the synthesized CDs in polymer matrix (such as polyvinyl pyrrolidone [3], silica gel [4], agar [5], silicone [6]) to preserve surface states, but such physical dispersion of colloidal CDs in polymer matrix may be incompatible with semiconductor processes compared with conventional rare-earth phosphors. Moreover, these white LEDs were used costly deep-blue or UV LEDs as the excitation sources [3–5], or used CDs and toxic semiconductor quantum dots composites as phosphors [6]. It is meaningful to develop easy and low-cost methods to obtain white LEDs with light blue LEDs and solid-state CDs phosphors without luminescence quenching.

In this letter, we fabricate CDs and CDs-polymer composite in one step using hexamethylenetetramine (HMT) and polycarbonate as precursors by a facile hydrothermal method. The growth temperature is as low as 150 °C. The precursor of polycarbonate has the advantages of easy manufacture and low cost. To the best of our knowledge, polycarbonate has not been used as precursor to synthesize solid-state luminescent CDs. The synthesized composite powders were easily cut or polished into phosphors, which is compatible with the manufacturing process of phosphors. We also demonstrated solid-state white light emission by coating CDs-polymer composite powders onto a 455 nm blue LED.

HMT solution was prepared with the concentration of 2 wt%. 35 mL HMT solution and 2 g polycarbonate were placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The solutions were further purified via dialysis for a month in distilled water. All chemicals were purchased from Alfa Aesar.

We performed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements using an Escalab 250 spectrometer with an Al X-ray source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on JEOL, JEM-2100F at operating voltage of 200 kV. Infrared spectroscopy was performed using a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. Emission and excitation spectra were recorded using a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with Xe lamp as an excitation source.

As-prepared product looks turbid. After purification, the solution seems to separate into three layers, as show in Fig. 1 a. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of monodispersed CDs (2–6 nm) in limpid layer. The high resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c) shows crystalline structures with a lattice spacing of 0.227 nm, which is close to the (1 0 0) diffraction facet of graphite [7]. Fig. 1d shows the TEM image of large particles (5–10 µm) in turbid layer, indicating that some CDs adhere to large polymeric particles. The polymer comes from the esterification of polycarbonate with the produced ammonia by reacting HMT and water under hydrothermal condition [8]. Based on these findings, we speculate that the bottom precipitation is composed of CDs-polymer composites because the synthesis of CDs and precursor polymerization perform at the same time under hydrothermal treatment. The thermal decomposition of HMT is very slow during heating and we do not know whether this is responsible for the formation of CDs-polymer composites. We also explored growing them using ethanolamine, diethanol amine, or melamine instead of HMT via the same hydrothermal method, but failed. This makes us further convinced that it is the unique characteristics of HMT and polycarbonate leading to the formation of CDs-polymer composites.

Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectra of CDs. The absorption bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH2 groups. CDs were also investigated by XPS. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) presents three main peaks at 284.6, 286.1 and 288.0 eV, which are corresponding to the CC, COH and CN coordination, respectively [9]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be fitted to a main binding energy at 399.6 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N [10]. FTIR and XPS measurements indicate that CDs are nitrogen-doped with surface rich of amino-groups. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs excited by various incident lights are shown in Fig. 2d. When the excitation wavelength shifts from 375 to 475 nm, the emission intensity first increases and reaches a maximum at the excitation wavelength of 425 nm, and then decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength, but the emission peak positions remain unchanged. That is to say, the emission peaks of the CDs are excitation-independent. For colloidal CDs, the excitation-dependent emission was frequently reported, whereas excitation-independent luminescence was seldom achieved. In this letter, the excitation-independent PL behavior of the CDs should be attributed to the surfaces rich of amino-groups [11].

When the precipitation was filtrated and dried, yellow polymer-CDs composite powders were obtained. Fig. 3 a depicts the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the powders. There is strong light absorption in the visible range and optical band gap was estimated to be 2.27 eV. In the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of the powders (Fig. 3b), two peaks in UV and visible range are related to two kinds of electron transitions. Considering that most cost effective white LEDs normally use blue InGaN LED chips, we measured PL spectrum of the powders with the excitation wavelength of 455 nm (Fig. 3c). There is a broad yellow emission band peaked at 550 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL peak is as large as 100 nm and the PL quantum yield of the powders is up to 14.86%. Such a high QY implies the powders gain strong solid-state luminescence and have potential applications as yellow phosphors in white LEDs. Fig. 3d presents the time-resolved PL curve of the powders. The decay curve is well fitted with a single-exponential function, implying that only one emission state dominated the PL property. The fitting function gives a lifetime τ of 1.87 s, which is in the same order as the results of N-doped CDs reported before [5]. The short lifetime is indicative of the radiative recombination of the excitons.

Usually, blue LED chip combines with yellow phosphors can produce white light. We obtain white light emission by coating a 455 nm blue LED lens with yellow polymer-CDs powders, as shown in Fig. 3e. The corresponding electroluminescence (EL) spectrum consists of two parts including blue light from LED and yellow light from the powders. The 550 nm peak from the EL spectrum matches well with the PL peak in Fig. 3c. The working device emits white light at a typical power of 1 W (inset of Fig. 3e). The emission intensity has not changed significantly over a week of continuously lighting. The Commission International d'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinate was calculated to be (0.29, 0.38) from the emission curve (Fig. 3f). The color temperature corresponds to 7152 K. The coordinate is a little far from pure white coordinate (0.33, 0.33) compared with the previous reported results [3–5], but our white device has the advantages of easy manufacture and low cost besides of excellent luminescent stability. Moreover, highly white LEDs may be able to be obtained by optimizing the growth parameters of CDs-polymer composite. These CDs-polymer composite powders exhibit excellent light converting properties. The nontoxic nature will make this material promising for a wide range of optoelectronic devices.

In conclusion, we used HMT and polycarbonate as precursors and synthesized CDs-polymer powders with solid-state PL by a hydrothermal method. In comparison to the previous reports, three features become apparent: (1) CDs-polymer composites were synthesized in one step by a facile hydrothermal method. The growing temperature is as low as 150 °C. The precursor of polycarbonate is cost-effective; (2) CDs-polymer composite powders do not show any luminescence quenching. The quantum yield is as high as 14.86%; (3) white light emission was observed by coating a common 455 nm blue LED with CDs-polymer powders. This study provides a new potentiality and insight into metal-less white LED phosphors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61674096, 11704168), Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J17KB003).
